key_insight: The paper builds a generative pipeline that learns depth-dependent fiber misalignment statistics from X-ray-μCT data and uses them to synthesize realistic 3D composite microstructures.
engineering_relevance: It enables simulation-ready, statistically equivalent fiber architectures for virtual testing, design studies, and damage-analysis workflows in as-manufactured composites.
method: Extract per-fiber, per-slice in-plane and out-of-plane misalignment using slice-segment ellipse intersection.
Fit a stochastic model with copula-based dependence, latent autoregressive continuity, and rare extreme-misalignment motifs.
Generate 3D microstructures via variable-radius seeding, slice-by-slice growth, and Delaunay/ellipse contact resolution to prevent overlap.
evidence_signal: The optimized model matched the original statistical descriptors closely, with deviations generally below 10%, and generated about 2400 synthetic fibers while preserving strong fidelity to the μCT data.
watch_item: The approach depends on careful calibration of hyperparameters and robust geometric contact handling, so transfer to different materials or imaging conditions may require re-tuning.
paper_title: Autoregressive Modelling and Synthetic Generation of High-Fidelity, Statistically Equivalent 3D Microstructures for As-Manufactured Misalignments in Fiber-Reinforced Composites
